body:
Let \(\mathbf{r}(t)=\langle x(t),y(t),z(t)\rangle\) be a differentiable position vector of a particle. The **velocity** is the first derivative \(\mathbf{v}(t)=\mathbf{r}'(t)=\langle x'(t),y'(t),z'(t)\rangle\). The **acceleration** is the second derivative \(\mathbf{a}(t)=\mathbf{r}''(t)=\langle x''(t),y''(t),z''(t)\rangle\).  

*Example (3‑D projectile):* With initial speed \(v_0\) at elevation angle \(\theta\) and azimuth \(\phi\), and gravity \(\mathbf{g}=\langle0,0,-g\rangle\),
\[\mathbf{r}(t)=\langle v_0\cos\theta\cos\phi\,t,\;v_0\cos\theta\sin\phi\,t,\;v_0\sin\theta\,t-\tfrac12gt^2\rangle.\]
Differentiating gives \(\mathbf{v}(t)=\langle v_0\cos\theta\cos\phi,\;v_0\cos\theta\sin\phi,\;v_0\sin\theta-gt\rangle\) and \(\mathbf{a}(t)=\langle0,0,-g\rangle\).

The **speed** is the magnitude of velocity: \(|\mathbf{v}(t)|=\sqrt{(v_0\cos\theta\cos\phi)^2+(v_0\cos\theta\sin\phi)^2+(v_0\sin\theta-gt)^2}\). The **unit tangent** (direction of motion) is \(\mathbf{T}(t)=\frac{\mathbf{v}(t)}{|\mathbf{v}(t)|}\).

questions.q: Given \(\mathbf{r}(t)=\langle t^2,\sin t, e^t\rangle\), what is \(\mathbf{v}(t)\)?
questions.explanation: Differentiate each component: derivative of \(t^2\) is \(2t\), of \(\sin t\) is \(\cos t\), of \(e^t\) is \(e^t\).

questions.q: For the projectile example above, what is the acceleration vector \(\mathbf{a}(t)\)?
questions.explanation: Only gravity acts, giving a constant acceleration downward: \(\langle 0,0,-g\rangle\).

questions.q: If \(\mathbf{v}(t)=\langle 3\cos t, 3\sin t, 2\rangle\), what is the speed \(|\mathbf{v}(t)|\)?
questions.explanation: Compute magnitude: \(|\mathbf{v}|=\sqrt{(3\cos t)^2+(3\sin t)^2+2^2}=\sqrt{9(\cos^2 t+\sin^2 t)+4}=\sqrt{13}\).

title: Helix Visualizer
caption: Use sliders for parameters \(a\) (radius) and \(b\) (pitch) in the parametric equations \(x = a\cos t,\; y = a\sin t,\; z = b t\).

title: Ask the Tutor
greeting: Hi! I'm here to help you connect velocity and acceleration of vector functions to real‑world kinematics in robotics. Ask me anything about trajectory planning, speed profiles, or curvature!

title: LibreTexts – Velocity and Acceleration
relevance: Provides formal definitions of \(\mathbf{r}(t)\), \(\mathbf{v}(t)\), \(\mathbf{a}(t)\), speed, and the unit tangent vector.
